title: 100-Million-Year-Old Snake Fossil with Legs Rewrites Evolution Story
summary:
A remarkably preserved fossil from Argentina is revolutionizing our understanding of snake evolution. The specimen belongs to Najash rionegrina, an ancient snake that lived nearly 100 million years ago in what is now northern Patagonia. Unlike modern snakes, Najash had hind legs and a cheekbone called the jugal bone—a feature that has almost completely disappeared in living snakes.

To study the fossil without damaging it, researchers led by Fernando Garberoglio used micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) scanning. This technology allowed them to reconstruct the skull in exceptional detail, including the paths of nerves and blood vessels. What they found challenged a long-held belief about snake origins. "Our findings support the idea that the ancestors of modern snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed—instead of small burrowing forms as previously thought," Garberoglio explained.

The study, published in Science Advances in 2019, also revealed that early snakes retained their hindlimbs for an extended period before the rise of mostly limbless modern snakes. This corrects a 160-year-old anatomical error. "After 160 years of getting it wrong, this paper corrects this very important feature based not on guesswork, but on empirical evidence," said co-author Michael Caldwell.

Since the Najash discovery, other fossils have added twists to the story. In 2020, a fossil blind snake from Brazil, Boipeba tayasuensis, showed that some early blind snakes were over 1 meter long, much larger than their living relatives. A 2023 study suggested the ancestor of living snakes may have been adapted for burrowing but also opportunistic. And a 2025 Nature study described a Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland with a mix of lizard and snake traits, showing that early squamate evolution involved a lot of experimentation.

Despite these later discoveries, Najash remains one of the clearest windows into a crucial stage of snake evolution. It captures a moment when snakes still had hindlimbs, retained a more lizard-like skull, and had not yet fully acquired the body plan of their modern descendants. It's not just an ancient snake—it's an ancient snake in transition.
why_it_matters: This research demonstrates how modern imaging technology can overturn century-old textbook knowledge, and it shows that evolution is a process of constant tinkering – even snakes experimented with body plans before settling on their modern form.

keywords.explanation: A type of X-ray scanning that creates very detailed 3D images of the inside of an object without cutting it open.
The process by which living things change over many generations to better survive in their environment.
Information that comes from actual observation or experiments, not from guesswork.
The back legs of an animal.
A group of reptiles that includes lizards and snakes.
Relating to the physical structure of a body or its parts.

questions.question: What special feature did the ancient snake Najash rionegrina have that most modern snakes lack?
How did researchers study the fossil without damaging it?
What old idea about snake origins did this fossil challenge?
options: That snakes evolved from aquatic animals
That snake ancestors were small burrowing forms
questions.correct_answer: That snake ancestors were small burrowing forms

questions.question: How long had an anatomical error about snake skulls existed before this study?
Which other fossil snake was mentioned as being over 1 meter long?
What kind of animal was described in a 2025 Nature study that showed early squamate evolution involved experimentation?
options: A Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland
A Permian amphibian from South Africa
questions.correct_answer: A Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland

background_read: The fossil was found in northern Patagonia, a region in Argentina rich in Cretaceous deposits. Patagonia has yielded many important fossils of early mammals, dinosaurs, and reptiles that help scientists piece together the breakup of Gondwana.

Snakes belong to the reptile group called squamates (lizards and snakes). For over a century, paleontologists debated whether snakes evolved from burrowing ancestors or from large, surface-dwelling reptiles. Najash provides strong evidence for the latter.

Micro-CT (computed tomography) scanning is a non-destructive technique often used in paleontology to visualize internal structures such as brain cavities or the paths of nerves within fossils, without physically dissecting them.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Fossil Najash rionegrina reshapes understanding of snake evolution.
└─ Key features: Hind legs and a jugal (cheek) bone – absent in most modern snakes.
METHOD: Micro-CT scanning allowed 3D reconstruction of skull nerves and blood vessels.
FINDING: Challenged the 'small burrowing ancestor' hypothesis – suggests ancestors were big-bodied and big-mouthed.
CORRECTION: Fixed a 160-year-old anatomical error about snake skulls using empirical evidence.
├─ 2020: Boipeba tayasuensis – early blind snake over 1 m long.
└─ 2023 & 2025: Further studies showing burrowing+opportunistic origins and experimental early evolution.
SIGNIFICANCE: Najash is a transitional form – a snake caught in the act of losing its limbs and lizard-like skull.

perspectives.description: Paleontologists gain a clearer picture of snake origins, validating the 'big-bodied ancestor' hypothesis and providing a solid transitional fossil that connects early lizards and modern snakes.
The study relies on a single exceptionally preserved skull; more fossils are needed to confirm whether Najash truly represents the mainstream snake lineage or was an evolutionary dead end.

The pattern emerging from multiple discoveries (Najash, Boipeba, the Scottish squamate) suggests that early squamate evolution was highly experimental, with many forms coexisting before the modern snake body plan became dominant.

Future high-resolution CT scanning of other snake fossils could further refine the timeline of limb loss and skull modification, and may reveal even earlier transitional forms from the Jurassic period.
